Proper adherence to the manufacturer's instructions in specifying and installing the system is essential to achieving a performance level that complies with building code requirements.

In the context of building codes, if a dwelling's private curtilage includes a parking area not enclosed within a carport or garage, the parking bay must be designed to allow for a widened space of at least 3.3 metres to accommodate accessibility requirements.

If parking is located within the private curtilage of the dwelling but not under a carport or within a garage, at least one standard parking space must be provided that can be expanded to a minimum width of 3.3 metres.

If parking is located within the private curtilage of a dwelling but not inside a carport or garage, it must include at least one standard parking bay that has the potential to be expanded to a minimum width of 3.3 metres.
In building code terms, a carport is distinct from a standard parking bay, which may be widened to 3.3m to accommodate larger vehicles when located within the private curtilage of a dwelling.

If the parking area is located within the private curtilage of the dwelling but not inside a garage or carport, the requirements specify that at least one parking space must meet the dimensions of a standard parking bay.
A level difference greater than 20 millimetres is permissible at the threshold of an external door leading to an individual dwelling, provided it can be safely navigated with reasonable effort.

In building code terms, the access between a dwelling and its associated outside space must account for any level differences greater than 20 millimetres, provided they can be safely and practically managed at the door threshold.

If the level difference outside the door exceeds 20 millimetres, it may still be permitted provided that the slope or ramp required to eliminate it with equipment in the outdoor space can be achieved with reasonable effort.
